Activity Title: Creating New Year goal stars using card and collage materials
Learning Objectives: This activity gives children space to think about something they would like to learn or try. It supports early understanding of goals as “something I am working towards”. It also strengthens self-belief and helps children talk about what matters to them. Children practise expressing ideas verbally before putting them into marks, drawings or cut-out images. They make decisions about how to represent their goal and begin to consider small steps that might help them reach it. This activity works well at the start of January and can be revisited later in the term. When children see their own words displayed over time, the concept of growth becomes visible rather than abstract.
Observation Cues:
• Children can explain what they want to achieve in simple language
  • They use phrases such as “I’m learning to…” or “I want to…”
  • They show pride when sharing their star with the group
  • They problem-solve when cutting, sticking or choosing images
  • They refer back to their goal during later play
Materials:
1. Coloured card (pre-cut star shapes or full sheets)
  2. Pencils
  3. Pens
  4. Scissors
  5. PVA glue
  6. Glue spreaders
  7. Coloured paper
  8. Clipboards (optional for writing support)
  9. Cushions or small chairs for circle time
  10. Selection of magazines for cutting images
Step-by-Step Guidance: Prepare a quiet circle space with cushions or chairs. Place materials on a nearby table but keep them out of reach until discussion is finished.During circle time, introduce the idea of a goal as something we practise and work towards. Keep this concrete. Use examples such as learning to ride a bike, putting on a coat independently, or drawing a person with details.Invite each child to share one idea. Support turn-taking calmly. Scribe key phrases if needed.Move to the creative table. Offer children a star shape or allow older children to draw and cut their own.Children represent their goal by drawing, writing initial letters or words, or cutting images from magazines. Stay close enough to support, but allow decisions to remain theirs.When finished, invite children to briefly explain their star. Display the stars at child height so they can revisit them independently.
Adult Role:
• Be steady and neutral. The aim is reflection, not performance.
  • Model language such as “You’re learning to…” or “You’re practising…” rather than praising the goal itself. This keeps the focus on effort.
  • Support children who struggle to think of a goal by offering practical prompts based on what you have observed in recent weeks.
  • Scaffold fine motor skills without taking over. Guide hand positioning for scissors or offer thicker pens if grip is weak.
  • Support emotional regulation during sharing. Waiting for a turn may be challenging. Narrate what is happening so expectations remain clear.
  • Notice pride, hesitation or uncertainty and respond with calm acknowledgement rather than evaluation.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Keep the focus on simple intentions such as “I am learning to jump” or “I am learning to say please”. Adults may scribe the child’s words. Emphasise talking rather than writing. Cutting may be limited to tearing paper.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Encourage children to draw themselves achieving their goal. Introduce the idea of small steps. For example, “First I practise balancing.” Some may begin writing initial sounds.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Invite children to write a simple sentence independently. Encourage them to identify one practical step they can try this week. Revisit stars later in the term to reflect on progress.
Space Required: Small group circle space and one table for creative work.
Safety Considerations:
Ensure scissors are child-safe and appropriate to age

Check pencil tips are not excessively sharp

Supervise magazine cutting to avoid frustration or minor paper cuts

Keep glue lids secure when not in use
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Personal, Social &Emotional Development

Children build confidence as they articulate intentions and reflect on achievements. They practise turn-taking and emotional regulation during group discussion.

Communication & Language

Children explain their ideas, listen to others and use emerging vocabulary linked to learning and effort.

Physical Development

Cutting, drawing and sticking strengthen fine motor control.

Literacy

Older children experiment with mark making, initial sounds and simple sentence writing for meaning.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Prepare a calm, ordered workspace with limited materials. Offer real card, sharp but appropriate child scissors, and a small writing tray. Allow children to choose their working position and complete their star independently without group sharing if they prefer.The adult speaks minimally and observes closely. Instead of prompting goals verbally in a circle, present picture cards showing everyday skills and allow the child to select quietly. Notice concentration, independence and self-correction during cutting and writing rather than the content of the goal.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Take the activity outdoors. Replace card stars with star shapes drawn onto slices of wood or thick recycled card. Children can decorate using natural materials such as leaves, small sticks or mud marks.The adult shifts to a facilitative role, focusing on resilience and real-life challenge. Goals may centre on physical risk such as climbing higher or balancing on logs. Observe how children assess their own confidence and how they respond to small setbacks in the outdoor environment.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Transform the environment by creating a “goal studio” space with a mirror, photographs of children engaged in learning, and large paper stars displayed on the wall at working height.Invite collaborative discussion before individual creation. Document children’s words verbatim and display them beside their stars. The adult listens for deeper thinking and asks open prompts such as “What might help you?” Documentation becomes part of the environment so children revisit and expand their thinking over time.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Introduce the idea of goals through a short seasonal story about a character learning something new. Keep the rhythm gentle and unhurried.Provide natural materials such as thick card in muted colours and beeswax crayons. Avoid magazine imagery. The adult focuses on imagination and inner intention rather than written outcomes. Observe how the child expresses their goal through colour, symbol or simple drawing rather than literal wording.

Activity Title: Exploring Winter Emotions Through Body Mapping and Mark Making
Learning Objectives:
Children explore how winter makes their bodies feel and begin linking physical sensations (cold hands, shivering, warm cheeks) to emotions (excited, calm, uncomfortable). They use mark making to represent feelings and talk about what they notice.

‍
Observation Cues:
• Whether children can describe physical sensations in simple language
  • How children link body feelings to emotions
  • The vocabulary they already use for emotions
  • How confidently they use pencils and shapes
  • Whether children can recognise and name simple shapes
  • How they wait, listen and respond to others during sharing
  • This activity sits strongly within PSED, Communication and Language, Physical Development and early Literacy and aligns with winter learning across the yearly curriculum.
Materials:
1. Emotion cards (clear facial expressions)
  2. PencilsColoured paper or card
  3. Body outline diagrams (simple front-facing outline)
  4. Shape insets or stencils
  5. Clipboards (optional for floor work)
  6. Real winter clothing items (hat, scarf, gloves)
  7. Small baskets for sorting
Step-by-Step Guidance: Place body outline sheets on the table or floor space.Put pencils and shape insets nearby.Place emotion cards in a small basket within reach.Add a basket of winter clothing items beside the workspace.Begin by inviting children to handle the winter clothing. Allow them to put on a hat or scarf. Keep the tone observational:“You put the gloves on. What do your hands feel like now?”Introduce emotion cards gradually. Lay out three or four at first. Ask children to choose one that matches how winter feels to them.Move to the body outline. Encourage children to draw marks where they feel something in winter. For example, blue scribbles on hands for cold, circles on cheeks for warmth, zigzags in the tummy for excitement.Offer shape insets as an optional support. Model gently:“You chose a circle. Where could that go on the body?”Record children’s spoken language directly onto the page. Keep wording simple and accurate to what they say.Avoid correcting interpretations. The goal is connection and expression, not accuracy.
Adult Role:
• Stay emotionally present and neutral. Children are sharing internal experiences.
  • Model precise vocabulary: cold, shivery, tight, cosy, calm, excited.
  • Scaffold turn taking without rushing responses. Allow thinking time.
  • Support pencil grip only if needed. Focus on comfort rather than correctness.
  • Notice when a child struggles to link sensation and emotion. Offer prompts such as, “When your hands feel cold, do you feel happy, cross, or something else?”
  • Monitor safety when using pencils and ensure clothing items are clean and intact.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu:
Reduce emotion cards to two clear options such as happy and sad.
Focus on naming sensations rather than linking abstract emotions.
Use larger body outlines and chunky pencils.
Keep sessions short and movement-based.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi:
Encourage simple reasoning: “Why does winter make you feel that way?”
Introduce three to five emotion cards.
Support early shape recognition during drawing.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco:
Invite children to draw their own body outline.
Encourage written marks that represent words.
Ask children to compare: “Does everyone feel the same in winter?”
Support simple sentence attempts alongside drawings.
Space Required: Table space for 4–6 children or clear floor space with clipboards.
Safety Considerations:
Ensure pencils are appropriately sharpened

Check clothing items for loose threads or damage

Maintain hygiene if clothing is shared
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Personal, Social and Emotional Development

Children explore and name emotions linked to bodily experience.

Communication and Language

Children listen to others and extend vocabulary for feelings and sensations.

Physical Development

Fine motor control develops through controlled mark making and shape tracing.

Literacy

Early meaning-making through marks and adult scribing.

Understanding the World

Children reflect on seasonal change and how it affects their bodies. The EYFS seven areas of learnin…
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Use a limited set of high-quality, realistic emotion cards and real winter garments made from natural fibres. Present materials neatly on trays with defined spaces. Children choose independently and return items after use.The adult speaks minimally and avoids leading questions. Observation focuses on independence, careful handling of materials and whether the child self-initiates emotional language without prompting. Interruptions are avoided unless safety requires it.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Take the activity outdoors on a cold day. Replace printed body outlines with large chalk drawings on paving or bark. Use natural materials such as leaves or twigs to mark areas of the body that feel cold or warm.The adult draws attention to real environmental sensations — wind on cheeks, cold air in lungs. Observation focuses on resilience, risk awareness and how children regulate comfort by adjusting clothing themselves.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Create a larger shared body outline on display paper fixed to the wall. Add mirrors nearby so children can observe their own faces while discussing emotions. Include soft lighting and neutral background space to reduce distraction.The adult documents children’s language verbatim and displays it alongside the artwork. Discussion is collaborative. The adult revisits the documentation later, inviting children to reflect and extend ideas. Attention is placed on group meaning-making rather than individual worksheets.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Begin with a short seasonal story about winter wind visiting the body. Use soft natural paper and beeswax crayons in muted colours. Avoid printed emotion cards and instead describe feelings through imagery.The adult maintains a calm, rhythmic tone and allows silence between contributions. Observation centres on imaginative expression and how children represent inner experience symbolically rather than literally.

Activity Title: Exploring winter textures and small world play using a sensory tray
Learning Objectives:
This activity offers children repeated opportunities to explore cold, soft and natural textures while playing alongside others. It creates space for shared attention, early conversation and imaginative use of materials, with adults supporting language, turn-taking and emotional expression.

‍
Observation Cues: Adults may observe how long children remain engaged, which materials they return to, and how they use scoops or hands to move items. Pay attention to body language and facial expressions, especially when children encounter cold ice or negotiate space and resources. Notice the winter-related words children already use, how they communicate needs non-verbally, and how they respond when problems arise during shared play.
Materials:
1. Builders tuff tray or large activity tray
  2. Ice cubes (large enough to avoid choking risk)
  3. Cotton wool
  4. Natural resources (e.g. pinecones, smooth stones, twigs)
  5. Scoops or ladles
  6. Small world winter figures or animals
Step-by-Step Guidance: Begin with a clean, dry tray placed on the floor or a low surface. Add a simple base such as cotton wool to suggest snow and soften the space. Arrange natural resources and winter figures loosely rather than evenly spaced. Add ice cubes last so their temperature change becomes part of the experience.Invite children to approach in their own time. Allow them to touch, scoop, move and combine materials freely. Avoid demonstrating outcomes. Stay nearby to model calm handling of resources and to support shared use when several children are drawn to the same objects.
Adult Role: The adult stays emotionally present and observant, offering language that matches what the child is doing: naming textures, temperatures and actions without directing play. Support turn-taking by acknowledging feelings and describing what is happening, rather than solving conflicts immediately. Step in only when safety or emotional regulation requires it, then return control to the children as soon as possible.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl: Offer fewer materials at one time, with a focus on ice, cotton wool and one or two scoops. Adults stay close, naming actions such as “cold”, “wet”, “scoop” and “drop”, and supporting early sharing through parallel play.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Include small world figures to prompt early pretend play. Adults model simple phrases linked to winter and emotions, and support short turns with shared tools.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Add a wider range of natural materials. Adults invite thinking aloud with comments such as noticing melting ice or changes in texture, and observe how children negotiate roles and space together.
Space Required: A clear floor area large enough for several children to gather comfortably around the tray.
Time Required: 20–30 minutes, with flexibility for children who wish to stay longer.
Safety Considerations: Check all natural materials for sharp edges or damage. Ensure ice cubes and loose parts are large enough to avoid choking risk. Supervise closely, particularly when children are using scoops or when ice begins to melt.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Communication & Language

This activity links strongly to Communication and Language as adults model vocabulary and support shared interaction.

Physical Development

Builds on grasping, scooping and hand–eye coordination.

Understanding the World

Children explore through investigating natural materials and seasonal changes.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: The tray is prepared with a limited number of real, natural objects and one scoop per child. Materials are placed with clear spacing, inviting careful handling. The adult introduces the tray quietly, then observes without interruption, noting concentration, repeated actions and emerging independence. Language is offered sparingly and only when it reflects the child’s focus.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: The activity is taken outdoors using frost, leaves, mud and ice collected from the environment. The adult supports children to notice temperature, weather and seasonal change, while managing risk through close supervision. Attention is given to how children adapt play when materials behave unpredictably.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: The environment is adjusted to feel like an invitation, with the tray positioned near natural light and neutral backing materials. Children are encouraged to rearrange the space and combine items freely. The adult documents moments of collaboration or storytelling through notes or photographs, then responds in the moment by reflecting children’s ideas back to them.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): The tray is simplified using only natural fibres and muted colours. The adult’s voice remains slow and rhythmic, with minimal language. Observation focuses on mood, sensory response and the quality of imaginative play rather than verbal output.

Activity Title: Exploring winter textures through a sensory treasure basket
Learning Objectives: This activity offers children repeated opportunities to explore cold, soft and natural textures while playing alongside others. It creates space for shared attention, early conversation and imaginative use of materials, with adults supporting language, turn-taking and emotional expression.
Observation Cues: Adults may observe how long children remain engaged, which materials they return to, and how they use scoops or hands to move items. Pay attention to body language and facial expressions, especially when children encounter cold ice or negotiate space and resources. Notice the winter-related words children already use, how they communicate needs non-verbally, and how they respond when problems arise during shared play.
Materials:
• Medium wicker or soft fabric basket
  • Scarves (varied textures)
  • Soft winter hats
  • Wooden spoons
  • Pinecones (large, intact)
  • Natural sponges
  • Small piece of silver or metallic fabric
  • Floor mat or rug
Step-by-Step Guidance:
1. Place the basket on a soft mat in a quiet, warm space.
  2. Sit nearby at the child’s level without crowding them.
  3. Allow the child to approach the basket independently.
  4. Resist handing items immediately. Wait for the child to reach.
  5. When the child picks up an object, offer calm commentary:
    • “Soft scarf.”
    • “Cold spoon.”
    • “You’re squeezing the sponge.”
  6. Mirror facial expressions and respond to sounds.
  7. If interest fades, gently rotate one item back into view rather than introducing something new.

The basket remains open-ended. There is no expectation to use objects in a particular way.
Adult Role:
The adult’s primary role is emotional regulation and language modelling. Stay physically close but not intrusive.

Use simple, concrete language describing what the child is doing. Avoid quizzing or directing (“What is it?” “Can you find…?”).

Notice when the child needs reassurance and respond with tone and presence. Ensure items remain safe and intact throughout play.

The adult names textures, temperatures and actions without overstimulation. The focus is shared attention, not performance.
Age Adaptation: 0–12 months (Little Lear: Place the basket within reach during tummy time or supported sitting. Limit the contents to 4–5 larger items. Focus on secure attachment. The adult mirrors expressions and keeps language rhythmic and repetitive. Objects may be explored with the mouth; hygiene and safety are prioritised.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl: Increase the number of items slightly. Children may begin transferring objects in and out of the basket. Add simple action words: “pull,” “drop,” “shake.” Allow children to carry items short distances. Support early independence while staying emotionally available.
Space Required: Quiet indoor floor space large enough for one or two children to sit comfortably.
Safety Considerations: Supervise at all times. Check pinecones for sharp edges or loose pieces. Ensure all items are large enough to prevent choking. Remove any damaged materials immediately. Wash fabric items regularly.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Physical Development

Children practise grasping, squeezing, transferring and reaching, strengthening fine motor control and coordination.

Communication and Language

Adult commentary supports early vocabulary and listening skills through repeated exposure to simple descriptive language.

Personal, Social and Emotional Development (PSED)

Calm shared exploration builds security, confidence and early independence.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori:
Use a low shelf rather than a basket so each item is clearly visible and spaced apart. Limit materials to high-quality natural objects with clear sensory differences. The adult steps further back physically, allowing uninterrupted concentration. Language is precise and minimal. Observation focuses on repetition and the child’s independent choice-making rather than shared interaction.

‍
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Move the activity outdoors on a dry blanket. Replace scarves and hats with natural winter finds such as bark, evergreen sprigs and smooth stones. The adult draws attention to temperature differences in real air conditions. Observation focuses on how children respond to natural sensory variation such as breeze, cold and uneven textures.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Arrange the basket near natural light with a neutral backdrop to emphasise texture and shadow. Add a mirror panel behind the objects so children can see movement and fabric shimmer. The adult documents which materials attract longest engagement using brief notes or photographs. During play, the adult responds to gestures as communication, treating the child as an intentional explorer. The environment is adjusted over time based on observed interests.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Select muted, natural fibres such as wool and cotton. Reduce the number of objects and introduce them slowly. The adult uses gentle, rhythmic language and slower movements. Attention is placed on maintaining a calm atmosphere and predictable flow rather than varied stimulation.

Activity Title: Melting ice to rescue winter animals using pipettes and warm water
Learning Objectives:
This activity gives children repeated chances to notice cause and effect as they apply warm water to ice and watch it change. They experience the difference between solid and liquid in a concrete way. They practise controlled hand movements while using pipettes. They explore simple ideas about animal habitats in winter.

‍
Observation Cues:
• Children noticing that “more water” speeds up melting
  • Language such as “cold”, “frozen”, “melting”, “water”, “ice”
  • Attempts to solve the problem in different ways
  • Fine motor control when squeezing and releasing pipettes
  • Cooperative behaviour when sharing bowls or animals
  • Emotional responses to waiting, success, or frustration
Materials:
1. Builders tuff tray
  2. Small world animals (wildlife or winter animals)
  3. Activity trays or plastic containers for freezing
  4. Two bowls of warm water
  5. Pipettes
  6. Natural materials such as leaves and twigs
  7. Towels or cloths for spills
Step-by-Step Guidance: Place natural materials across the base of the tuff tray to create a simple winter landscape.Position the frozen animal blocks around the tray within easy reach.Place pipettes around the tray edge. Keep bowls of warm water on a nearby table to reduce spills.Gather children briefly and explain that the animals are trapped in ice. Demonstrate drawing water into the pipette and gently releasing it onto the ice.Step back. Allow children to choose an ice block and begin exploring.Model language sparingly: “The ice feels hard.” “The water is warm.” “It is starting to melt.”Once animals are freed, encourage children to place them into the winter landscape and talk about where they might live.Keep the pace unhurried. The waiting is part of the learning.
Adult Role:
• Stay emotionally available and observant. Offer help only when needed.
  • Model precise language linked to what children can see and feel. Avoid abstract explanations.
  • Support turn-taking where necessary. Narrate problem-solving attempts without taking control.
  • Monitor temperature and comfort. Ensure children’s hands are not becoming too cold.
  • Notice effort rather than speed. The focus is exploration, not rescue time.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl: Use larger ice blocks with partially exposed animals. Offer fewer materials. Focus on squeezing, pouring and feeling temperature differences. Keep language simple and repetitive.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Encourage simple problem-solving: “What could we try?” Support early imaginative play once animals are freed. Model short two- to three-word phrases.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Invite prediction: “What do you think will happen if we add more water?” Introduce vocabulary such as “solid” and “liquid” in context. Encourage cooperative rescue attempts.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Extend into discussion about habitats and seasons. Ask children to explain their thinking. Introduce measuring language such as “faster”, “slower”, “more”, “less”.
Space Required: Table space for a builders tuff tray and a nearby surface for warm water bowls.
Time Required: 30–45 minutes, depending on ice size and group engagement.
Safety Considerations:
Check water is warm, never hot.

Mop spills immediately.

Monitor children for cold hands.

Demonstrate safe pipette use to prevent snapping or finger pinching.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Understanding the World

Children observe changes in materials and explore seasonal context.

Physical Development

Squeezing pipettes strengthens fine motor control and hand–eye coordination.

Communication & Language

Children describe what they see and hear new vocabulary modelled in context.

PSED

Turn-taking and managing frustration support emotional development.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Prepare a defined workspace with individual trays rather than one shared tuff tray. Limit each child to one ice block, one pipette and one bowl of warm water. Use real ceramic or glass bowls where safe, to reinforce care of materials.The adult demonstrates the full sequence slowly once, then withdraws. Interruptions are minimised. The adult observes grip control, independence, and whether the child returns materials to their place without prompting.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Run the activity outdoors using natural frost or ice where possible. Replace pipettes with small natural scoops or collected shells. Use water warmed in insulated flasks.The adult supports safe risk-taking in cold conditions. Attention is given to weather, changing temperature and the natural environment. Discussion centres on real seasonal changes rather than small world play alone.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Create an inquiry table with light beneath a clear tray so children can see the ice changing from different angles. Add clipboards and pencils nearby for mark-making. Display photographs of ice, frost and winter habitats at child height.The adult listens for theories about why the ice melts. Children’s ideas are documented with notes or photographs and revisited later. The environment remains flexible and may be adjusted based on children’s emerging questions.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Begin with a short seasonal story about animals resting beneath winter ice. Use muted, natural fabrics beneath the ice blocks. Keep materials simple and limited.The adult maintains a calm rhythm in speech and movement. Focus remains on sensory experience and imagination rather than scientific explanation. Children are allowed extended quiet time to observe the slow melting process.

Activity Title: Observing and recording daily weather using clipboards and weather symbols
Learning Objectives:
• Children explore how to identify and describe weather using simple vocabulary.
  • They notice seasonal patterns and talk about change over time.
  • They make marks with meaning as they record what they observe.
  • They sort and match weather symbols to what they can see in the sky.
Observation Cues:
• Use descriptive words such as sunny, cloudy, windy, cold or warm
  • Notice differences across days (“It was raining yesterday”)
  • Attempt to represent weather through lines, symbols or early writing
  • Choose to work alone, alongside others, or collaboratively
  • Sustain attention outdoors and return to the task independently
Materials:
• Paper
  • Pencils
  • Clipboards
  • Binoculars

Additional resources:

  • Weather symbol cards (sun, rain, wind, snow, clouds)
  • Interactive weather board or simple display chart
  • Tablet or printed weather images (optional reference only)
  • Cushions or outdoor seating area
Step-by-Step Guidance: Prepare a comfortable base area with cushions indoors or outdoors.Place clipboards, paper and pencils on a table for children to collect independently.Lay out weather symbol cards clearly on a mat or easel.Introduce the activity by inviting children to step outside and “check the sky.”Model looking carefully. Use binoculars to focus attention upwards and across the horizon.Ask simple noticing prompts such as “What can you see?” or “Is the sky the same colour everywhere?”Return to the base area and invite children to choose a weather card that matches what they observed.Encourage children to record what they noticed using marks, drawings, or early letters.Add their chosen symbol or drawing to the interactive weather board.Keep the pace steady. Allow children to revisit the observation more than once in the same session.
Adult Role:
• Stay alongside rather than directing.
  • Model precise vocabulary without overloading language.
  • Repeat and extend children’s words (“Yes, it is cloudy. The clouds are thick and grey.”).
  • Encourage mark making without correcting form.
  • Support turn-taking around binoculars and shared materials.
  • Maintain awareness of weather conditions and adapt if needed.
  • Notice who observes carefully, who prefers movement, and who returns to add detail later.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl:
Focus on sensory noticing rather than recording.
Go outside briefly and name what can be felt: wind on cheeks, warmth on hands.
Offer large crayons instead of pencils.
Adults scribe single words while children watch.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu:
Introduce two clear weather choices at a time.
Encourage simple marks such as dots for rain or lines for wind.
Use repetition daily to build confidence in naming.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi:
Invite comparison with yesterday.
Encourage children to explain their choice of symbol.
Begin simple sorting such as grouping sunny days together.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco:
Introduce a weekly weather chart.
Encourage early phonetic spelling of weather words.
Invite children to predict tomorrow’s weather and explain their thinking.
Space Required: Access to a safe outdoor area plus a calm indoor or sheltered base space for recording.
Time Required: 15–25 minutes, depending on discussion length and age group.
Safety Considerations:
Check outdoor conditions before beginning. Avoid high winds or unsafe weather.

Ensure pencils are appropriately sharpened.

Model safe handling of clipboards.

Supervise binocular use and outdoor boundaries.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Communication and Language

Children develop vocabulary through real observation and discussion.

Understanding the World

Children notice seasonal change and explore the natural environment directly.

Literacy

Children make marks with meaning and begin linking symbols to spoken words.

Mathematics

Sorting and categorising weather symbols introduces early data handling.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Prepare a small, ordered weather station with real tools only. Limit symbol cards to the current season to reduce distraction. Place clipboards neatly on a tray for independent collection.Stand back after the initial introduction. Avoid prompting once a child is engaged. Observe how they categorise weather without correction. Pay attention to repetition and independence in returning to the task daily.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Move the entire activity outdoors. Use natural markers such as sticks to draw symbols in soil before transferring to paper. Encourage children to feel wind direction and notice changes in light.Adopt a slower pace. Allow managed risk such as standing in light rain. Observe resilience and problem-solving in adapting to weather conditions rather than focusing on the finished recording.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Create a visible documentation wall showing previous days’ weather recordings. Add photographs of the sky taken by children. Arrange materials attractively to invite discussion.Position yourself as a co-researcher. Ask open questions that extend thinking. Record children’s explanations alongside their drawings. Notice how their theories about weather change over time.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Introduce the activity through a short seasonal verse or simple rhythmic chant. Use soft-coloured pencils and natural materials. Keep visual displays uncluttered.Use gentle, descriptive language rather than direct questioning. Pay attention to mood and atmosphere as much as vocabulary. Observe how children express weather through colour and movement as well as drawing.

Activity Title: Practising dressing skills using real winter clothing
Themes: winter; autumn; dressing; sorting; role-play; outdoor-exploration; problem-solving; all-about-me
Learning Objectives:
This activity gives children repeated chances to practise dressing skills using real outdoor clothing. It supports growing independence, body awareness, and confidence in caring for themselves.

‍
Observation Cues:
• Attempting to put items on in different ways before asking for help
  • Persisting with fastenings or abandoning them quickly
  • Naming items and body parts, or using gesture to communicate intent
  • Watching or copying peers, offering help, or negotiating turns
  • Showing frustration, pride, or satisfaction through facial expression and posture
Materials:
A selection of winter clothing from the setting:

  • Coats
  • Hats
  • Scarves
  • Gloves
  • Wellies

Use items children are already familiar with.

Avoid adding novelty clothing that distracts from the focus on self-care.
Step-by-Step Guidance:
Gather all outdoor winter clothing in one place. Lay items flat on the floor, place them in a builder’s tuff tray, or hang them at child height on a dressing-up rail.

Invite children to explore freely. Do not demonstrate unless a child asks or becomes stuck.

Allow children to dress and undress themselves, try on items that do not “fit”, or wear clothes in unconventional ways. This exploration is part of the learning.

Keep the area open for sustained play. Children may return to the activity several times.
Adult Role:
• Stay emotionally available and observant.
  • Acknowledge effort more than outcome.
  • Model simple, functional language linked to what the child is doing: naming clothing items, body parts, and actions.
  • Offer help only when it is clearly needed, and do so slowly so the child remains involved.
  • Support social interaction by noticing sharing, waiting, and helping between children.
  • Hold the boundary around safety while keeping the atmosphere calm and unhurried.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl: Children may insist on doing it themselves while needing reassurance. Support emotional regulation when frustration appears.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Children may insist on doing it themselves while needing reassurance. Support emotional regulation when frustration appears.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Introduce gentle prompts such as “What goes first?” or “Where does that part go?” to encourage sequencing and problem-solving.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Children may manage full outfits and support peers. Notice growing independence and sustained focus.
Space Required: A clear indoor floor area with enough room for clothing to be spread out and children to move safely.
Time Required: 20–30 minutes, with flexibility for children to dip in and out.
Safety Considerations: Check all clothing before use. Avoid broken zips, sharp fastenings, or tight elastic that could pinch skin. Ensure scarves and cords are supervised closely to reduce entanglement risk.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Physical Development

Children practise fine motor control, coordination, and self-care through real dressing actions.

Personal, Social & Emotional Development

The activity supports confidence, independence, and awareness of self, while offering opportunities for cooperation and empathy.

Communication & Language

Children hear and use language linked to everyday actions, objects, and body awareness.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori:
Offer a limited selection of complete, well-fitting winter clothing, laid out neatly and consistently in the same order each time. Items are placed where children can reach them independently, with no duplicates and no excess choice.

The adult demonstrates dressing actions slowly and precisely if invited, then steps back. Attention is on how the child sequences actions, persists with difficulty, and manages fastenings. The adult intervenes only to protect safety or when frustration clearly blocks progress.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School:
Arrange the clothing as a visual system rather than a pile. Hang coats at child height, place shoes directly below, and include a mirror positioned so children can see their whole body while dressing. Leave clear space between items so relationships are readable.

The adult observes how children interpret the layout: whether they follow the visual order, move between related items, or use the mirror to adjust clothing. Language responds to these choices by naming connections the environment suggests, rather than giving instructions.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia:
Set the activity up close to the exit or outdoors, using the clothing children will actually wear next. Items may be placed on benches, logs, or mats, reflecting the outdoor setting and weather conditions.

The adult supports dressing as part of preparing to go outside, not as a separate task. Attention is on how children respond to cold, wind, or wet ground, and how this affects their motivation to dress, persist, or seek help.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf):
Use a small range of natural-fibre clothing in muted colours. Lay items out calmly and consistently, avoiding visual clutter. The pace of the environment is slow and predictable.

The adult uses a steady, rhythmic voice and minimal language, often narrating actions rather than questioning. Attention is on the child’s emotional state, noticing when calm repetition supports confidence or when the rhythm needs to pause to reduce frustration.

Activity Title: Printing repeating snowflake patterns using paint and stamps
Learning Objectives:
This activity gives children repeated opportunities to explore pattern, shape and colour variation through stamping. It supports early mathematical thinking as children notice repetition, spacing and symmetry. It also strengthens fine motor control and encourages descriptive language linked to winter.

‍
Observation Cues:
• Whether children repeat a stamp in a line, row or cluster
  • How children experiment with spacing, rotation and overlap
  • Use of vocabulary such as “same”, “different”, “next”, “bigger”, “light”, “dark”
  • Control when holding and pressing the stamp
  • Whether children mix colours intentionally or by accident
  • Collaboration, turn-taking or negotiation over materials
Materials:
1. Coloured card or coloured paper
  2. Paint
  3. Paintbrushes
  4. Paint pots or paint trays
  5. Pencils (for older children planning patterns)
  6. Messy mat or table covering
  7. Activity trays (to contain materials)
  8. Bowl of warm water for washing stamps
  9. Snowflake sponges or stamps
  10. Printed reference images of real snowflakes
Step-by-Step Guidance: Cover the table with a messy mat and place paper at each setting.Prepare paint. Offer one colour in three tones (light, medium, dark) or two contrasting colours. Keep quantities small to avoid flooding.Place stamps on an activity tray. Position paint trays within easy reach.Introduce the activity briefly. Show how to dip, press, and lift without sliding. Avoid demonstrating a finished example.Allow children to begin freely.Comment on what you see rather than directing: “You’ve printed three in a row.” “That one looks darker.”Encourage washing stamps between colours if children choose to change.Offer additional paper when needed so children can continue without pressure to “finish”.Keep the pace calm. The focus is repetition and noticing, not producing a display piece.
Adult Role:
• Stay close and observant.
  • Model precise language linked to shape, pattern and shade.
  • Avoid correcting layout or symmetry.
  • Support children who press too lightly or overload paint by showing the movement slowly again.
  • Notice when a child begins to create a sequence. Gently name it without turning it into a task.
    For example: “You’ve made light, dark, light.”
  • Encourage turn-taking if resources are limited, but ensure enough materials are available to prevent unnecessary conflict.
  • Maintain responsibility for paint quantities, water spills and hygiene.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Offer one colour in two tones only. Use larger stamps. Focus on pressing and lifting. Adult language centres on simple pattern words such as “again” and “same”.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Introduce conversation about snowflakes being unique but often symmetrical. Invite children to notice whether their prints look balanced or scattered. Encourage simple repeating patterns across the page.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Provide pencils for light planning lines or borders. Children may experiment with deliberate AB or AAB patterns. Adults can introduce the idea of symmetry by folding paper lightly to guide placement if the child shows interest.
Space Required: Table space for small groups of 4–6 children. Easy access to water for cleaning stamps.
Time Required: 30–40 minutes, with flexibility for repetition over several sessions.
Safety Considerations:
Ensure paints are non-toxic and washable.

Monitor water bowls to prevent spills.

Remind children not to put sponges in mouths.

Clean surfaces promptly to reduce slipping.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Expressive Arts and Design

Children explore colour, texture and print-making techniques.

Mathematics

Children notice patterns, repetition, position and shape through stamping.

Communication and Language

Adults model descriptive vocabulary linked to colour and sequence.

Physical Development

Repeated pressing strengthens hand control and coordination.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Limit the table to one carefully prepared paint tray with graded tones of a single colour. Offer a small number of high-quality stamps arranged neatly on a tray. The adult demonstrates once, slowly, then steps back.During play, the adult avoids commentary unless invited. Attention is given to concentration and repetition. If a child repeats the same motion many times, this is respected rather than redirected.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Move the activity outdoors on a sturdy table or low surface. Replace commercial stamps with found natural objects arranged in snowflake-like shapes. Use diluted paint or natural pigment if available.The adult supports children to experiment with pressing uneven textures. Attention is given to how natural materials print differently. Mess and weather become part of the experience rather than something to control.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Change the environment so the printing table sits near a window with real snowflake photographs displayed at child height. Add mirrors flat on the table so children can see reflections of their prints, inviting conversation about symmetry.The adult documents children’s emerging patterns through photos or short quotes. Dialogue is encouraged between peers: “What do you notice about theirs?” The focus shifts toward shared meaning-making rather than individual output.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Use soft winter tones such as blues and muted purples, blended wet-on-wet on thicker paper. Avoid defined stamp shapes initially and allow colour to spread gently.The adult speaks minimally and works rhythmically. Instead of focusing on pattern language, attention is placed on the sensory experience of colour meeting water. The process is valued over recognisable shape.

Activity Title: Sorting and counting foam snowballs by size using number cards
Learning Objectives: This activity gives children repeated opportunities to notice quantity, compare size and use number language in a hands-on way. It supports early mathematical thinking while also requiring turn taking and patience.
Observation Cues: Recognising and matching numerals to quantity Counting with one-to-one correspondence Comparing objects using size language (small, medium, large) Waiting, sharing and negotiating space Practitioners should notice: Which numerals children recognise independently Whether they count accurately or skip objects The size language children naturally use (small, tiny, big, huge) How they respond when waiting for a turn Signs of pride, frustration or persistence during problem-solving
Materials:
• Number cards 0–10 (extend to 20 for confident counters)
  • Foam balls in three clearly different sizes (small, medium, large)
  • Four baskets or buckets
  • Labels: small, medium, large
  • Coloured sorting bowls (optional for clearer visual grouping)
Step-by-Step Guidance: Place three empty baskets in a row. Label each clearly: small, medium, large.Fill the fourth basket with a mixed collection of foam “snowballs”.Introduce the activity calmly. Tip the mixed basket slightly so children can see the range of sizes.Model sorting first. Pick up one ball and say, “This one feels small. I’m putting it in the small basket.”Invite children to sort independently. Avoid correcting immediately. Watch their decision-making first.Once sorting begins, introduce number cards. Place a card next to a basket and count the snowballs together, touching each one as you count.For confident children, ask them to find a specific number of snowballs and place them into a basket.Keep the pace steady. Allow repetition. Remove number cards if children become overwhelmed and return to pure sorting.
Adult Role:
• Stay observant before intervening. Notice how children categorise and whether they adjust when comparing two objects side by side.
  • Model precise language without over-explaining. Use phrases such as “This one is larger than that one” or “We have five small snowballs. Let’s check again.”
  • Support turn taking by narrating the process rather than instructing. “You’re waiting while Sam finishes. Your turn is next.”
  • Step in only if conflict escalates or counting becomes frustrating. Offer calm redirection rather than correction.
  • Maintain safety by ensuring snowballs are not thrown and that all materials remain on the floor or table.
Age Adaptation: 12–24 months (Early Expl: Focus purely on filling and emptying baskets. Remove number cards. Use simple language such as “big ball” and “little ball.” Counting is rhythmic rather than accurate.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Introduce two size categories instead of three. Count up to five together. Emphasise turn taking and naming size words.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Use all three size categories. Introduce number cards to 10. Encourage children to check quantities independently.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Extend to 20. Ask children to predict which basket will have the most. Introduce simple comparison language such as “more than” and “fewer than.”
Space Required: Floor space or a low table large enough for four baskets and shared movement around them.
Time Required: 15–25 minutes depending on engagement and group size.
Safety Considerations:
Ensure foam balls are too large to be swallowed

Remind children snowballs stay in hands or baskets

Monitor movement around baskets to prevent pushing
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Mathematics

Children practise counting, recognising numerals and comparing size and quantity.

Personal, Social and Emotional Development

Children take turns, manage frustration and build confidence through success in sorting and counting.

Communication and Language

Adults model descriptive vocabulary and comparative language during play.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Reduce the materials to one tray with three clearly defined sections instead of separate baskets. Present the activity on a low shelf and allow one child to work with it at a time. Use real woven baskets rather than plastic where possible.The adult demonstrates slowly once, then steps back. Correction happens indirectly through comparison rather than instruction. The practitioner observes whether the child self-corrects when sizes are placed side by side and only intervenes if the material is being misused.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Take the activity outdoors. Replace foam balls with natural objects such as pinecones, stones or bundled leaves. Remove printed labels and invite children to decide their own categories based on size or weight.The adult allows uneven ground and natural challenges to remain part of the experience. Attention is given to how children manage risk, carry objects and negotiate space. Counting may happen while crouching, standing or balancing on logs.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Arrange the baskets in a visually intentional way, perhaps on a large sheet of white paper. Provide clipboards and pencils nearby for children who wish to draw or record their findings. Invite children to discuss their reasoning. “Why did this go here?” Display sorted collections and photograph them to revisit later. The adult listens for mathematical language emerging naturally and documents children’s thinking rather than steering it.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Use natural wool balls in soft winter tones rather than bright foam. Introduce the activity with a short seasonal story about gathering snowballs before they melt.The adult keeps a gentle rhythm to the counting. Language remains simple and sensory. Observation focuses on the child’s mood, steadiness and engagement rather than numerical accuracy.

Activity Title: Storytelling with winter puppets and props in a small group
Learning Objectives: Children use puppets and simple props to retell or invent a winter story within a small group. This gives repeated chances to practise sequencing, extend vocabulary, listen to others, and build confidence speaking in front of peers.
Observation Cues:
• Whether children recall phrases or key events from the original story
  • How they sequence events (beginning, middle, end — even loosely)
  • The vocabulary they choose, especially winter language
  • How they negotiate roles and share puppets
  • Who prefers to observe before joining in
Materials:
• Hand puppets (animals, people, or winter characters)
  • Puppet stand or table-top stage
  • Cushions or small chairs
  • Basket for puppets
  • Basket for props
  • Small world animals
  • Coloured card (for simple backdrops)
  • Pens or pencils (for adults to note key phrases)
  • Optional: builders tuff tray as a defined “stage” area
Step-by-Step Guidance: Prepare a defined storytelling space.Arrange cushions or chairs in a semi-circle. Position the puppet stand so all children can see clearly.Place puppets and props in baskets behind the stand.Keep them visible but not yet in children’s hands.Briefly revisit the chosen winter story.This may be a familiar book or a simple adult-told narrative about snow, animals, or cold weather.Model a short section using one puppet.Keep it concise. Emphasise expressive language and clear sequencing.Invite children to choose a puppet.Limit numbers if needed to avoid overwhelm. Support turn-taking calmly.Step back.Allow children to retell, adapt, or completely reinvent the story. Intervene only to support language, inclusion, or safety.Close by briefly reflecting together.“What happened at the start?”“Who helped in the snow?”Keep this reflective moment short and concrete.
Adult Role:
• The adult creates psychological safety first. Some children will speak immediately. Others need time to watch.
  • Model rich but simple language. Repeat key phrases from the story without correcting children’s variations. Expand sentences gently:
  • Child: “Cold bear.”
  • Adult: “Yes, the bear felt very cold in the snow.”
  • Support turn-taking without forcing participation. If conflict arises over puppets, narrate the problem and offer solutions rather than imposing them.
  • Observe who leads, who follows, and who withdraws. Adapt future sessions accordingly.
  • Remain aware of physical safety around the stand and props.
Age Adaptation: 2 years (Budding Adventu: Keep the story structure simple and repetitive. Focus on single events rather than full narratives. Use strong gestures and repeated phrases. Expect parallel play before cooperative storytelling.
Age Adaptation: 3 years (Curious Investi: Encourage children to add new events. Prompt lightly with “What happened next?” Support cooperative storytelling and shared problem-solving between characters.
Age Adaptation: 4 years (Confident Disco: Invite children to create their own winter storyline from scratch. Introduce simple challenges: “The snow blocked the door. What could they do?” Encourage sustained dialogue between characters.
Space Required: Small defined group area with clear visibility. Quiet enough to hear voices comfortably.
Time Required: 15–25 minutes depending on age and engagement.
Safety Considerations:
Check puppets for loose threads or detachable parts.

Ensure the stand is stable and cannot topple.

Avoid long cords, ribbons, or scarves that could wrap around necks.

Supervise closely during transitions between children.
EYFS (2024) Links with Explanation:
Communication and Language

Children practise listening, turn-taking, and extending spoken sentences.

Literacy

Children begin to understand how stories are structured and retold.

Personal, Social and Emotional Development

Children negotiate roles, manage frustration, and experience being heard within a group.

Expressive Arts and Design

Children use imaginative play and voice to represent ideas.
Ethos Adaptation: Montessori: Prepare a limited, carefully ordered set of puppets and props on a low shelf before the session begins. Each puppet has a defined place. The environment is calm and uncluttered. Children choose independently and return items to the same position after use.The adult avoids directing the storyline. Instead of prompting events, they observe which child is ready to lead. Language is clear and measured. The adult pays attention to independence — who selects thoughtfully, who cares for materials, who sustains focus without adult input.
Ethos Adaptation: Forest School: Move storytelling outdoors. Sit in a circle, ideally near natural winter features such as frost, bare branches, or fallen leaves. Use natural objects as props alongside puppets — sticks become trees, stones become caves.The adult’s voice adjusts to the outdoor space. They allow pauses to notice wind, sounds, or temperature. Observation focuses on resilience and sensory awareness — how children respond to cold air, uneven ground, and shared storytelling in an open environment.
Ethos Adaptation: Reggio Emilia: Rearrange the space so the backdrop becomes part of the learning. Use coloured card to create a simple winter scene designed collaboratively with children beforehand. Display children’s drawings of snow, trees, or animals around the storytelling area. The environment signals that children’s ideas matter.During storytelling, document key phrases or interactions. The adult listens closely for emerging themes and may revisit these later in drawings or mark-making. Attention is placed on collaboration — how children co-construct the narrative and build on one another’s ideas.
Ethos Adaptation: Steiner (Waldorf): Dim lighting slightly indoors or gather in soft natural light. Use natural fibre puppets in muted tones. Begin with a short seasonal verse or rhythmic introduction before the story.The adult tells the story slowly and rhythmically before inviting children to retell. Emphasis is placed on imagery rather than problem-solving. The adult observes depth of imaginative engagement and sustained attention rather than accuracy of retelling.
